title: Almond baklava
subtitle: Crisp filo layers with almond and walnut filling, drenched in honey syrup
excerpt: A Middle Eastern and Levantine confection of paper-thin filo pastry layered with a spiced almond and walnut filling, baked until golden and crisp, then immediately drenched in a warm honey and rosewater syrup. The syrup soaks into the layers and the baklava sets as it cools to a dense, sticky richness. Cut into diamond shapes before baking so the syrup can penetrate the cuts.
sourceNotes: Baklava is documented in Ottoman, Arab, and Levantine food literature from at least the fifteenth century. Muhammed bin Mahmud Shirwani, a fifteenth-century Ottoman physician and food writer, describes layered nut pastries with honey. Baklava in its current filo-layered, syrup-soaked format is described in Ottoman palace cookery records and regional cookbooks from the nineteenth century, now in the public domain, including Türabi Efendi's Turkish Cookery Book (1862) and later Levantine and Greek domestic guides.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baklava improves after 24 hours as the syrup distributes evenly through the layers. Keeps at room temperature, loosely covered, for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Low, steady heat browns the filo evenly without scorching the top layers before the interior heats through. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: 30 x 40 cm tin that matches the filo sheet size as closely as possible.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pouring hot syrup onto hot baklava produces a soft, soggy result; pouring cold syrup onto hot baklava produces a greasy one. The correct method is to pour the warm syrup onto the freshly baked, still-hot baklava as soon as it comes out of the oven, then leave untouched while both cool. The temperature differential creates capillary action that draws the syrup deep into the cut edges rather than pooling on the surface. Make the syrup while the baklava bakes so it is ready at the right temperature when needed.

content.text: Cut the baklava into diamonds before it goes in the oven, all the way through to the base, so the syrup can soak into the layers.
is the other key: pour hot syrup over hot baklava the moment it comes out, then leave it alone to cool for at least 2 hours.

items.prepNote: about 20 sheets; keep covered with a damp cloth when not in use
finely chopped, for sprinkling on top

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Brush the tin generously with melted butter. Mix the ground almonds, walnuts, sugar, and cinnamon together. Lay a filo sheet in the tin and brush with melted butter. Repeat for 8 to 10 sheets, brushing each. Spread half the nut mixture evenly over the top. Add 5 more filo sheets, each brushed with butter. Spread the remaining nut mixture. Finish with the remaining filo sheets, brushing each including the top.

With a very sharp knife, cut the baklava all the way through into diamond shapes: first cut parallel strips about 4 cm apart, then cut diagonally across at the same spacing. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until deep golden.

While the baklava bakes, combine the sugar, water, honey, and lemon juice in a small saucepan. Bring to the boil, stirring until the sugar dissolves, then simmer for 5 minutes. Remove from heat and stir in the rosewater. As soon as the baklava comes out of the oven, pour the hot syrup evenly over the top. Scatter with chopped pistachios. Leave at room temperature, uncovered, for at least 2 hours before serving.
